definition: To bless is to draw favor down upon a person, object, or act — to sanctify by word and gesture, invoking a higher power's protection or grace. It differs from consecrating: to consecrate sets a thing wholly apart as sacred, while to bless confers benefit and goodwill, a benediction that can fall on a candle, a field, a departing soul, or a meal.
history: Astonishingly, the root is bloody: Old English *blēdsian*, 'to consecrate with blood,' from *blōd* — a pagan rite of sprinkling an altar with sacrifice. The turn came with Christianity, which redirected the word to translate Latin *benedicere*, 'to speak well of,' so a splash of gore became a spoken benediction. The medieval Church built, as Keith Thomas notes, 'a comprehensive range of formulae' to draw God's blessing onto salt, water, candles, and stones — Klaassen records the blessing of a stone to restore its prelapsarian virtue. The blood drained out of the word entirely; only the favor remained. Magic keeps the martial-devotional sense — blessings that shield and strengthen.

def_sources.label: Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic (formulae to draw down blessing)
Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic (blessing of a stone)

etymology.origin: Old English blēdsian 'to consecrate with blood', from blōd 'blood'
etymology.note: Astonishingly, to bless once meant to sprinkle with sacrificial blood; Christianity redirected its sense toward Latin benedicere ('speak well of').

flavor_snippets.html: In truth, her own faith was gone, trodden in Ulamog's wake. She pantomimed the <mark>blessing</mark> in the hope that it would inspire others to continue to struggle.
The haunted blade shattered, and the geist drifted gratefully to the <mark>Blessed</mark> Sleep.
"Avacyn's protection is everywhere. From the holy church to the sacred glade, all that we see is under her <mark>blessed</mark> watch."

"The venom cleanses the sickness from your body, but it will not be pleasant, and you may not survive. Pharika's <mark>blessings</mark> are fickle."
—Solon, acolyte of Pharika

flavor_snippets.card: Seraph of New Capenna // Seraph of New Phyrexia
flavor_snippets.html:
The angels had vowed to protect New Capenna. She was determined to uphold that vow, no matter the cost.
With her Halo reserves exhausted, she began to change, and she made a new promise to <mark>bless</mark> New Capenna with glistening perfection.

May your bark be worse than your bite.
—Raccoonfolk war <mark>blessing</mark>

"Jade-touched, wisteria-crowned, with gifts of gold and the Harvest's <mark>blessing</mark>, return this honored one to us as sibsig."
—Priests' chant

"To join our ranks, one must be pure. To be pure, one must be <mark>blessed</mark> by etherium. To be <mark>blessed</mark>, one must forget oneself."
—Ethersworn prayer

"Our soldiers are <mark>blessed</mark> with lizard scales as hard and cold as steel."
—Sidar Jabari

"The call of the <mark>Blessed</mark> Sleep is not so strong as the call to protect those in need."
—Saint Traft
"I fell to my knees. I called out to Sigarda. And then I saw my tormentors blaze with <mark>blessed</mark> sunlight."
The Great Furnace's <mark>blessing</mark> is a spectacular sight, but the best view comes at a high cost.

"In this <mark>blessed</mark> structure let the very walls baptize themselves in the blood of intruders."
—Stronghold architect, journal

"It is our duty to bring the <mark>Blessed</mark> Sleep to the dead, even if they resist that gift."
—Grete, Order of Saint Traft

This creature can't attack or block unless you have the city's <mark>blessing</mark>.
